title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 10 (Fragment 4)
text:
It was neither. It was Rinaldo. The uncles must have left the Scriptorium, for Rinaldo was mak-ing a bonfire of spells in the middle of the yard. Domenico, Carlo and Luigi were busily carrying armfuls of scrips, envelopes and scrolls down from the gallery. Paolo recognized, already curling among the flames, the army-charms he and the other children had spent such a time copying. It was a shocking waste of work.

“This is what the Petrocchis have forced us to!” shouted Rinaldo, striking an attitude beside the flames. It was evidently part of his plan to enlist the young ones.

Paolo was glad to see Antonio and Uncle Lorenzo hurry out of the Saloon.

“Rinaldo!” shouted Antonio. “Rinaldo, we’re worried about Umberto. We want you to go to the University and enquire.”

“Send Domenico,” said Rinaldo, and turned back to the flames.

“No,” said Antonio. “You go.” There was something about the way he said it that caused Rinaldo to back away from him.

“I’ll go,” said Rinaldo. He held up one hand, laughing. “I was only joking, Uncle Antonio.”

He left at once: “Take those spells back,” Uncle Lorenzo said to the other three cousins. “I hate to see good work wasted.” Domenico, Carlo and Luigi obeyed without a word. Antonio and Uncle Lorenzo went to the bonfire and tried to stamp out the flames, but they were burning too strongly. Paolo saw them look at one another, rather guiltily, and then lean forward and whisper a spell over the fire. It flicked out as if it had been turned off with a switch. Paolo sighed worriedly. It was plain that no one in the Casa Montana could drop the habit of using spells. He wondered how long it would be before the enemy enchanter noticed.

“Fetch a light!” Antonio shouted to Domenico. “And sort out the ones that aren’t burned.”

Paolo went back to the kitchen before they asked him to help. The bonfire had given him an idea.

“There is quite a bit of mince,” Rosa was saying. “Dare we try with that?”

“Why don’t you,” said Paolo, “take the food to the dining room? I’ll light a fire there, and you can cook it on that.”

“The boy’s a genius!” said Marco.

They did that. Rosa cooked by relays and Marco made cocoa. The children were fed first, Paolo included. Paolo sat on one of the long benches, thinking it was almost enjoyable—except if he thought of Tonino, or Old Niccolo in bed upstairs. He was very pleased and surprised when a sudden bundle of claw and warm-iron muscle landed on his knee. Benvenuto was missing Tonino too. He rubbed against Paolo with a kind of desperation, but he would not purr.

Rosa and Marco were getting up to put the young ones to bed, when there was a sudden great clanging, outside in the night.

“Good Heavens!” said Rosa, and opened the yard door.

The noise flooded in, an uneven metal sound, hasty and huge. The nearest— clang-clang-clang —was so near that it could only be the bell of Sant’ Angelo’s. Behind it, the bell of the Cathedral tolled. And beyond that, now near, now faint and tinny, every bell in every church in Caprona beat and boomed and clashed and chimed. Corinna and Lucia came racing in, their faces bright with cold and excitement.

vocabulary.definition: A settled way of thinking or feeling about something.
vocabulary.example: ” shouted Rinaldo, striking an attitude beside the flames.

vocabulary.definition: A large open-air fire used for burning waste or as part of a celebration.
vocabulary.example: The uncles must have left the Scriptorium, for Rinaldo was mak-ing a bonfire of spells in the middle of the yard.

vocabulary.definition: Made a deep, loud, resonant sound.
vocabulary.example: And beyond that, now near, now faint and tinny, every bell in every church in Caprona beat and boomed and clashed and chimed.

vocabulary.definition: To make melodious ringing sounds.
vocabulary.example: And beyond that, now near, now faint and tinny, every bell in every church in Caprona beat and boomed and clashed and chimed.

vocabulary.definition: A loud, resonant metallic sound.
vocabulary.example: Rosa and Marco were getting up to put the young ones to bed, when there was a sudden great clanging, outside in the night.

vocabulary.definition: To make a loud, harsh noise by striking together.
vocabulary.example: And beyond that, now near, now faint and tinny, every bell in every church in Caprona beat and boomed and clashed and chimed.

vocabulary.definition: A curved pointed horny nail on each digit of the foot in birds, lizards, and some mammals.
vocabulary.example: He was very pleased and surprised when a sudden bundle of claw and warm-iron muscle landed on his knee.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows despair or great urgency.
vocabulary.example: He rubbed against Paolo with a kind of desperation, but he would not purr.

vocabulary.definition: A state of despair, typically one which results in rash or extreme behavior.
vocabulary.example: He rubbed against Paolo with a kind of desperation, but he would not purr.

vocabulary.definition: A person who uses magic or sorcery.
vocabulary.example: He wondered how long it would be before the enemy enchanter noticed.

vocabulary.definition: A person who is actively opposed or hostile.
vocabulary.example: He wondered how long it would be before the enemy enchanter noticed.

vocabulary.definition: To secure the support and help of someone.
vocabulary.example: It was evidently part of his plan to enlist the young ones.

vocabulary.definition: To ask for information.
vocabulary.example: We want you to go to the University and enquire.

vocabulary.definition: Clearly; obviously.
vocabulary.example: It was evidently part of his plan to enlist the young ones.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows one knows they have done something wrong.
vocabulary.example: Paolo saw them look at one another, rather guiltily, and then lean forward and whisper a spell over the fire.

vocabulary.definition: A regular practice or tendency.
vocabulary.example: It was plain that no one in the Casa Montana could drop the habit of using spells.

vocabulary.definition: A band or bundle of fibrous tissue in a human or animal body that has the ability to contract.
vocabulary.example: He was very pleased and surprised when a sudden bundle of claw and warm-iron muscle landed on his knee.

vocabulary.definition: To follow commands or instructions.
vocabulary.example: ” Domenico, Carlo and Luigi obeyed without a word.

vocabulary.definition: A system or arrangement whereby a job or task is performed by a series of people or groups in succession.
vocabulary.example: Rosa cooked by relays and Marco made cocoa.

vocabulary.definition: A room set apart for writing, especially in a monastery where manuscripts were copied.
vocabulary.example: The uncles must have left the Scriptorium, for Rinaldo was mak-ing a bonfire of spells in the middle of the yard.

vocabulary.definition: Causing intense surprise or dismay; very bad or wrong.
vocabulary.example: It was a shocking waste of work.

vocabulary.definition: To speak or call out loudly.
vocabulary.example: ” shouted Rinaldo, striking an attitude beside the flames.

vocabulary.definition: To eliminate or extinguish completely.
vocabulary.example: Paolo was glad to see Antonio and Uncle Lorenzo hurry out of the Saloon.

vocabulary.definition: (of a bell) to sound loudly and slowly.
vocabulary.example: Behind it, the bell of the Cathedral tolled.

vocabulary.definition: The use or expending of something carelessly, extravagantly, or to no purpose.
vocabulary.example: It was a shocking waste of work.
